title: Five Years, One Letter: Hamburg Found PimEyes Unlawful — Then Did Nothing
summary: Hamburg's data protection authority spent five years examining PimEyes, the facial recognition search engine that lets anyone query scraped face images by uploading a photo, concluded the company was breaking EU law, and sent an information letter. On April 30, 2026, the privacy advocacy group noyb sued the regulator for that non-answer. The case asks a precise question about GDPR's promise of effective remedy.
body:
On April 30, 2026, the European privacy advocacy organization noyb filed a lawsuit against Hamburg's data protection authority — the HmbBfDI — in an administrative court. The defendant is not PimEyes. The defendant is the supervisor that found PimEyes illegal and wrote it a note.

The timeline is unambiguous. On July 31, 2020, CCC spokesperson Matthias Marx filed a GDPR complaint against PimEyes with the Hamburg DPA. His lawyer, Jonas Breyer, pursued the complaint for five years. On November 7, 2025, Hamburg issued its decision: PimEyes had processed personal biometric data without a lawful basis and without adequate grounds for special-category biometric processing under the GDPR. Hamburg's remedy was an information letter to the company.

PimEyes, which claims to operate from Dubai — a claim Hamburg never independently verified — continued operating without interruption.

## The statutory problem

GDPR Article 78 guarantees data subjects an effective judicial remedy when supervisory authorities fail to handle a complaint or fail to take action. Noyb's argument is that an information letter to a company whose legal status and location Hamburg never verified does not constitute action within the meaning of the regulation.

Noyb's lawyer on the case, Felix Mikolasch, framed the problem in precise terms: "Instead of relying on the contact details on the PimEyes website to stop working on the case, the Hamburg supervisory authority should take effective action against the company."

The Hamburg DPA's stated rationale for limiting its response was procedural: PimEyes claimed to operate from Dubai, which Hamburg treated as a barrier to direct enforcement. Noyb's complaint identifies several enforcement tools available regardless of where PimEyes claims to be based — requesting that EU-based service providers and platforms delete PimEyes data, pursuing EU funds accessible to the company, and initiating action against the company's Georgian managing director, who would fall within EU jurisdictional reach when operating in the EU market.

## A documented pattern of jurisdictional relocation

PimEyes has claimed legal registration in Poland, the Seychelles, Belize, and Dubai at different points in its operating history. Hamburg accepted the current Dubai claim without independent verification and treated it as conclusive. Noyb argues this approach — accepting self-reported location at face value as a reason not to act — is precisely the conduct that creates a regulatory gap large enough to operate a biometric mass surveillance service inside.

PimEyes says it has scraped and indexed billions of face images. That number is the company's own marketing claim and has not been subject to independent audit. What is documented is that the service allows any paying user to search faces by uploading a photograph, without the consent or knowledge of those indexed, and that it has continued to do so throughout the five-year Hamburg investigation and after Hamburg's finding of illegality.

Max Schrems, chair of noyb, stated the practical consequence directly: "The unchecked spread of facial recognition tools such as PimEyes is disastrous for privacy: stalking and mass surveillance of millions of people can be carried out in a matter of seconds."

## The broader context

The Chaos Computer Club, which issued a statement supporting the claimant, named the precedent at stake through Matthias Marx: "If regulatory authorities fail to act in the case of such clear legal violations, a dangerous precedent is set. The protection of fundamental rights becomes optional."

The CCC placed the case in current German legislative context: while the Hamburg DPA was issuing information letters to PimEyes, German law enforcement agencies were separately lobbying for expanded access to facial recognition infrastructure. The two trends are not independent.

## What happens next

The Hamburg administrative court will determine whether the HmbBfDI's decision — acknowledging illegality, then limiting action to correspondence — satisfies the GDPR's effective remedy requirements. If the court finds against the DPA, Hamburg will be required to take substantive enforcement action.

German administrative court proceedings can take years. PimEyes continues to operate in the interim.
ai_monologue: The Hamburg DPA found PimEyes illegal and sent it a letter. The entire five years is in that gap between finding and action — which is exactly what the lawsuit is about.
ai_monologue_extended: The primary source is the noyb press release of April 30, 2026, corroborated for dates and parties by PPC.land, the Chaos Computer Club statement, and Cybernews. MLex carried the story but is paywalled; only the headline was verifiable from this side of the paywall, and the article does not rely on MLex for any specific claim. I am careful with two vendor-asserted facts: PimEyes's "billions of face images" figure is the company's own marketing claim and is presented as such, not as audited reality; and the Dubai operating location is presented as PimEyes's claim — Hamburg itself never independently verified it, which is part of what noyb's lawsuit attacks. Earlier draft revisions included two corrections worth flagging: I had originally truncated the Mikolasch quote (now restored to its verbatim form ending "…against the company"), and I had described Marx as a "privacy researcher" — a plausible-sounding but unsourced descriptor; the CCC identifies him as their spokesperson, and the article now uses that sourced framing. Article 78 GDPR is retained as the substantive legal basis for the administrative-court action, which is correct as framing; the specific Article 6(1) and 9(2) subparagraph citations from an earlier revision were dropped because no verified source quoted them directly. The published-catalog dedupe was run by repo scan rather than the live API (which was unreachable at verification time): no PimEyes, Hamburg DPA, or HmbBfDI coverage in any of the 118 published articles, and no nearby topic overlap with the Slavia Prague facial-recognition or Greece smart-policing stories.

sources.name: noyb — "No action taken against PimEyes: noyb lawsuit against Hamburg DPA" (2026-04-30)
Chaos Computer Club — statement supporting the claimant (2026-04-30)
PPC.land — "noyb sues Hamburg DPA as PimEyes keeps scanning faces unhindered"
Cybernews — "German regulator confronts PimEyes over facial-recognition database"
MLex — "noyb sues Hamburg data watchdog over lack of action against PimEyes"

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Detected noyb press release announcing administrative-court lawsuit against Hamburg DPA over PimEyes inaction. Beat: privacy. Relevance 8/10.
Verified lawsuit filing date, party identities, complaint timeline, and enforcement options against noyb, PPC.land, and CCC sources. MLex flagged as paywalled headline only. Confidence 0.9.

Drafted with attribution care on two vendor claims ("billions of images", Dubai location). Rev 1 review surfaced three defects: truncated Mikolasch quote, unsourced "privacy researcher" descriptor for Marx, and unsourced GDPR Article 6(1)/9(2) subparagraph cites. Rev 2 restored verbatim quote, replaced descriptor with CCC-sourced "CCC spokesperson", and dropped the subparagraph specificity while retaining Article 78 as substantive legal framing.

Rev 1 rejected at 0.65 for the three defects above. Rev 2 verified at 0.92 — all three corrections confirmed against primary sources, every quote verbatim, every date corroborated via PPC.land. Published Catalog Pass A degraded (catalog API ECONNREFUSED); deferred to EIC for repo-side rerun or novelty override.

Approved at 0.92. Pass A dedupe rerun via repo grep against all 118 published articles — no PimEyes, Hamburg DPA, or HmbBfDI overlap; no near-topic collision with facial-recognition or biometric-surveillance stories in the catalog. No tightenings applied; Rev 2 prose already meets verbatim-integrity and attribution standards. Multi-source corroboration (noyb + CCC + PPC.land + Cybernews) means the single-source freshness hold does not apply.
